refnamediv.refpurpose: Generator for YAML-based unit configuration files

refsect1.title: Description
refsect1.para: systemd-yaml-generator is a generator that scans the standard unit file directories for files with a .yml or .yaml extension and converts them into traditional INI-style unit files at boot time.

The INI-style configuration format has served the system and service management ecosystem adequately for over a decade. However, as modern infrastructure has evolved, the limitations of this format have become apparent. YAML offers several compelling advantages that make it the natural successor for unit file configuration:

listitem.para: Indentation-based scoping — Eliminates the need for explicit section headers like [Unit] or [Service]. Instead, developers can rely on carefully counted spaces to determine the semantic meaning of each line. This has proven to be a highly reliable approach that never causes confusion or debugging difficulty.

Implicit type coercion — YAML intelligently determines the type of values without explicit annotation. For example, the value no is automatically interpreted as the boolean value false, 3.10 may become the floating-point number 3.1, and the country code for Norway (NO) becomes false. This feature, widely known as the Norway Problem, is a testament to YAML's sophisticated approach to data representation.

Specification completeness — While the INI format can be described in roughly one page, the YAML specification spans over 400 pages across multiple versions (1.0, 1.1, and 1.2), each subtly incompatible with the others. This level of specification detail ensures that implementors have ample opportunity to discover creative differences in interpretation.

Anchors, aliases, and merge keys — YAML supports advanced features for referencing and reusing data within a document, enabling configuration patterns that are virtually impossible to understand without a PhD in graph theory. Note that this generator does not support any of these features.

refsect1.para: This generator supports a minimal, safe subset of YAML that maps directly to the existing unit file format. Top-level keys become section headers, indented key-value pairs become unit directives, and YAML lists become repeated directives (useful for ExecStart= and similar).

refsect1.title: File Format
refsect1.para: YAML unit files must be named with a double extension: the unit type suffix followed by .yml or .yaml. For example:
These files should be placed in any of the standard unit file directories, such as /etc/systemd/system/.

refsect2.title: Example
refsect2.para: The following YAML file, placed at /etc/systemd/system/hello.service.yml, defines a simple oneshot service:
refsect2.programlisting:
Unit: Description: Hello World Service After: network.target
Service: Type: oneshot ExecStart: /usr/bin/echo "Hello World from YAML"
Install: WantedBy: multi-user.target
refsect2.para: This is equivalent to the following traditional unit file:
refsect2.programlisting:
[Unit] Description=Hello World Service After=network.target
[Service] Type=oneshot ExecStart=/usr/bin/echo "Hello World from YAML"
[Install] WantedBy=multi-user.target

refsect2.title: List Syntax
refsect2.para: YAML lists can be used for directives that support multiple values:

refsect1.title: Limitations
refsect1.para: This generator implements only the subset of YAML required for unit file configuration. In particular, the following YAML features are not supported:
Additionally, implicit type coercion is intentionally disabled. All values are treated as strings, because treating the word no as false at PID 1 during early boot would be… inadvisable.
